topics.title: The Preamble
topics.description: The soul of the Indian Constitution

lessons.title: What is the Preamble?
lessons.content: The Preamble is the introductory statement of the Constitution. It was described by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar as 'the key to open the minds of the makers.' It declares India to be a Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic.
keyPoints: Contains the objectives of the Constitution
Not enforceable in court but guides interpretation

lessons.title: Key Words in the Preamble
lessons.content: SOVEREIGN means India is free from external control. SOCIALIST means reducing inequality and providing basic needs. SECULAR means no state religion. DEMOCRATIC means government by elected representatives. REPUBLIC means elected head of state.
keyPoints: Sovereign: No external authority over India
Socialist: Economic and social justice for all
Secular: Equal respect for all religions
Democratic: People are the source of power

lessons.title: Overview of Fundamental Rights
lessons.content: Part III of the Constitution (Articles 12-35) guarantees six fundamental rights. These are justiciable rights — meaning you can go to court if they are violated. They protect individuals against the arbitrary actions of the State.
keyPoints: 6 Fundamental Rights exist (originally 7 — right to property removed in 1978)
Article 32 — 'heart and soul' of the Constitution
Can be suspended during National Emergency (except Art. 20 & 21)

lessons.title: Right to Equality (Articles 14-18)
lessons.content: Article 14 guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws. Article 15 prohibits discrimination based on religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. Article 16 ensures equal opportunity in public employment. Article 17 abolishes untouchability. Article 18 abolishes titles.
keyPoints: Art. 16: Equal opportunity in public employment

lessons.title: Right to Freedom (Articles 19-22)
lessons.content: Article 19 gives 6 freedoms: speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, and profession. Article 20 protects against ex-post facto laws and double jeopardy. Article 21 protects life and personal liberty. Article 21A gives right to education. Article 22 gives protection from arbitrary arrest.
keyPoints: Art. 21: Right to life and personal liberty

lessons.title: Right against Exploitation (Articles 23-24)
lessons.content: Article 23 prohibits trafficking of human beings and forced labour (begar). Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, and other hazardous jobs.
keyPoints: Art. 23: No trafficking, no forced labour
Art. 24: No child labour (below 14) in hazardous work

lessons.title: Right to Freedom of Religion (Articles 25-28)
lessons.content: Article 25 gives freedom of conscience and right to profess, practise and propagate religion. Article 26 gives freedom to manage religious affairs. Article 27 says no taxes for promotion of religion. Article 28 says no religious instruction in State-funded schools.
keyPoints: Art. 27: No tax for religious promotion
Art. 28: No religious instruction in government schools

topics.title: Fundamental Duties
topics.description: The responsibilities of every Indian citizen (Article 51A)

lessons.title: What are Fundamental Duties?
lessons.content: Fundamental Duties were added by the 42nd Amendment (1976) based on the Swaran Singh Committee recommendations. Article 51A lists 11 duties. They are non-justiciable (cannot be enforced in court) but serve as moral obligations.

lessons.title: Structure of Parliament
lessons.content: Parliament consists of the President, Lok Sabha (House of the People), and Rajya Sabha (Council of States). Lok Sabha has 543 elected members (+ 2 Anglo-Indians, now abolished). Rajya Sabha has 245 members (233 elected + 12 nominated). Parliament meets in the Central Hall of Parliament House in New Delhi.

lessons.title: Lok Sabha vs Rajya Sabha
lessons.content: Lok Sabha is the lower house, directly elected by people every 5 years. Rajya Sabha is the upper house, permanent body (never dissolved), with 1/3rd members retiring every 2 years. In case of conflict, Lok Sabha's will prevails in most matters. Only Lok Sabha can introduce Money Bills.
keyPoints: Lok Sabha: 5-year term, can be dissolved
Money Bills: Only in Lok Sabha

topics.title: The Judiciary
topics.description: Supreme Court, High Courts, and the judicial system

lessons.title: Supreme Court of India
lessons.content: Established under Article 124, the Supreme Court is the apex court of India. The Chief Justice of India heads it. It has original, appellate, and advisory jurisdiction. Its decisions are binding on all courts. It acts as the guardian of the Constitution.
keyPoints: Chief Justice of India — highest judicial officer
Judges: CJI + up to 33 judges (raised to 34 in 2019)
Writs under Article 32: habeas corpus, mandamus, etc.

lessons.title: Writs — Your Legal Shield
lessons.content: Writs are written orders from Supreme Court or High Court. Habeas Corpus: 'produce the body' — against illegal detention. Mandamus: 'we command' — orders public official to do their duty. Prohibition: stops lower courts from exceeding jurisdiction. Certiorari: quashes orders of lower courts. Quo Warranto: challenges authority of a person to hold public office.
keyPoints: Prohibition: Stop lower court from exceeding power
Quo Warranto: Challenge public office holder

topics.title: Emergency Provisions
topics.description: When the Constitution suspends normal functioning

lessons.title: Three Types of Emergencies
lessons.content: Article 352: National Emergency — war, external aggression, or armed rebellion. Article 356: President's Rule (State Emergency) — failure of constitutional machinery in a state. Article 360: Financial Emergency — threat to financial stability of India.

topics.title: Constitutional Amendments
topics.description: How the Constitution changes and evolves (Article 368)

lessons.title: Amendment Process
lessons.content: Article 368 governs constitutional amendments. Three methods: Simple majority (some provisions), Special majority (2/3 of members present and voting + majority of total strength of each house), Special majority + ratification by half the states (federal provisions).
keyPoints: Special majority + state ratification: Federal provisions
Kesavananda Bharati (1973): Basic structure cannot be amended

lessons.title: Key Amendments
lessons.content: 1st Amendment (1951): Exceptions to freedom of speech. 42nd Amendment (1976): Added 'Socialist', 'Secular', 'Integrity' to Preamble. 44th Amendment (1978): Restored rights curtailed by 42nd. 73rd/74th Amendments (1992): Gave constitutional status to Panchayats and Municipalities. 86th Amendment (2002): Right to Education.

scenarios.title: Religious Discrimination in Admission
scenarios.description: A private school funded by government grants denies admission to a student solely because of their religion.
scenarios.explanation: Article 15 prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. Since the school receives government funding, it is subject to this restriction.

scenarios.title: Arrest Without Information
scenarios.description: Police arrest a person at midnight and refuse to tell them why they are being arrested or allow them to speak to a lawyer.
scenarios.explanation: Article 22 guarantees that every arrested person must be informed of the grounds of arrest, produced before a magistrate within 24 hours, and given the right to consult a lawyer.

scenarios.title: Child Labour in Factory
scenarios.description: A factory owner employs 12-year-old children in a chemical manufacturing plant.
scenarios.explanation: Article 24 explicitly prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or any hazardous employment. This is a Fundamental Right, making it directly enforceable.

scenarios.title: Government Blocks News Channel
scenarios.description: A state government orders a news channel to stop broadcasting reports critical of the Chief Minister.
scenarios.explanation: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees freedom of speech and expression, which includes freedom of the press. The State cannot restrict this except on grounds mentioned in Article 19(2) — sovereignty, security, public order, decency, or contempt of court.

scenarios.title: State Imposes Religion in Schools
scenarios.description: A state government makes it mandatory for all government school students to attend daily prayers of a particular religion.
scenarios.explanation: Article 28 states that no religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds. This directly violates the secular principle.
